title: Surviving an Oil Spill in Louisiana, and More New Fiction for July
gist: Two July novels dramatize resource-curse and hustle economics—Soileau's Deepwater-Horizon-echoing Louisiana Cajun tale and Guns's Sri Lankan refugee family falling for a day-trading con—surfacing themes of oil dependence and scam-driven survival.

themes.theme: Oil and resource curse in fiction

insights.claim: Stephanie Soileau's 'Should the Waters Take Us' dramatizes a Deepwater-Horizon-like spill in a Louisiana Cajun community that is both economically dependent on and resentful of the oil industry, naming ExxonMobil repeatedly.
insights.speaker: Chloe Hadavas, Allison Meakem
insights.so_what: A cultural read on communities trapped by fossil-fuel dependence—the 'blessing and curse of oil' framing.

insights.claim: The novel contrasts intense US scrutiny of the Deepwater Horizon spill with the ignored, chronic 'every year an Exxon Valdez' pollution of Nigeria's Niger Delta.
insights.speaker: Chloe Hadavas, Allison Meakem
insights.so_what: Highlights the geographic double standard in environmental accountability for oil majors.

themes.theme: Hustle and scam economy in fiction

insights.claim: Priya Guns's 'Hustle, Baby' follows a Sri Lankan refugee family in Toronto racing to pay back rent, all falling victim to a con man's opaque day-trading scheme.
insights.speaker: Chloe Hadavas, Allison Meakem
insights.so_what: A cultural mirror of immigrant precarity and how financial-scam schemes prey on economic desperation.

insights.claim: The novel captures turn-of-millennium American life—megachurches, multilevel-marketing pink-Cadillac culture, and the early scam-infested internet—as backdrops to the survival grind.
insights.speaker: Chloe Hadavas, Allison Meakem
insights.so_what: Situates MLM and online-scam culture as enduring features of the modern economic hustle.
